sentence: Subjective:

The patient, a 53-year-old female, presents with a complaint of chest pain that she describes as dull and pressure-like, which occurs when she walks and has been ongoing for approximately three months.
The patient reports that the pain is relieved when she stops walking.
She denies having any shortness of breath, sweats, or clamminess.
Her past medical history includes diabetes and high blood pressure, which have not been previously diagnosed by our office but have been noted by other clinicians.
The patient is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily for her diabetes.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: Not measured in the office, but the patient's history of high blood pressure has been noted by other clinicians.
Pulse rate: Not mentioned.
Oxygen saturation: Not mentioned.
Temperature: Not mentioned.
Physical exam:
No jugular venous distension (JVD) or swelling in the neck.
Clear lung exam with no crackles or rhonchi.
Regular heart rate and rhythm, with a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
Equal pulses.
Soft and non-tender abdomen with no masses.
No back exam abnormalities.
Lower extremity exam reveals 1+ non-pitting edema in the ankle area with no calf tenderness.
Test Results:
ECG: Normal, no signs of a heart attack.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with chest pain that may be related to suspected high blood pressure and diabetes, with a possible diagnosis of unstable angina.
Problem list:
Diabetes mellitus; High blood pressure; Unstable angina (suspected).
Plan:
I prescribed aspirin 81mg daily for the patient's suspected unstable angina.
I also prescribed nitroglycerin 1-3 tablets every 5 minutes as needed for chest pain, with the instruction to seek immediate medical attention if the pain does not resolve.
I will refer the patient to cardiology for further evaluation and a stress test.
I will continue the patient's current metformin regimen and order another hemoglobin A1c test.
I will also start the patient on hydrochlorothiazide for her high blood pressure and instruct her to use a home blood pressure cuff and enter her readings in Epic MyChart.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with cardiology in one week for further evaluation and a stress test.
She will also return to our office in two weeks to review her hemoglobin A1c results and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
